channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Surge in Oil Prices Shakes Pakistan’s Already Fragile Economy
item.description: Families preparing for Eid al-Fitr and farmers ready for harvest are being squeezed in a country that gets nearly all its oil through the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A fuel storage site in Lahore, Pakistan, on Tuesday.

item.title: China’s Edge in an Oil Shock: Electric Cars and Renewables
item.description: Beijing’s decades-long push to reduce its dependence on foreign oil with huge investments in clean energy sources like electric vehicles is now paying off.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chinese demand for refined oil, gasoline and diesel fell last year as many people in China have switched to electric vehicles.

item.title: TikTok Investors Set to Pay $10 Billion Fee to Trump Administration
item.description: The large fee is the latest example of the White House’s inserting itself into corporate deal making in unusual and aggressive ways.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new investors paid the Treasury roughly $2.5 billion of the fee when the deal closed in January.

item.title: Jared Kushner Solicits Funds for His Firm While Working as Mideast Envoy
item.description: President Trump’s son-in-law is trying to raise $5 billion or more from foreign governments and others for his private equity firm.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Maureen Farrell
item.category: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.media:description: Jared Kushner, the founder of Affinity Partners, at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, in January.

item.title: The Fall of Noma’s René Redzepi Reverberates in the Restaurant World
item.description: In the industry where René Redzepi reigned, fellow chefs are debating how, and how much, restaurant kitchens can change.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Signe Birck for The New York Times
item.media:description: René Redzepi stepped down Wednesday as the chef of Noma, the restaurant that made Copenhagen a global dining destination, after he was accused of past physical abuse.

item.title: Chemical Smell at Control Center Halts Traffic at Washington and Baltimore Airports
item.description: The ground delays, which also affected the airport serving Richmond, Va., were expected to last until at least midnight, according to the F.A.A., which said the smell had affected air traffic controllers.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport was among the airports where the F.A.A. halted flights on Friday afternoon.

item.title: U.S. Vows to Block Iran’s Attempt to Shut Down Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz were “something we are dealing with.” And about 2,500 Marines were headed to the Middle East to bolster the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Luke Broadwater and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands marched amid airstrikes on Quds Day, a government-sponsored event, on Friday in Tehran.

item.title: Overlooked No More: Eleanor Abbott, the Creator of Candy Land
item.description: She invented the game nearly 80 years ago to distract children who were suffering in the hospital during a polio outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Sandra A. Miller and Margaret Muirhead

item.title: Are You Using A.I. at Work? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Please tell us how you’re using A.I. tools to fulfill your job responsibilities. We may include your experiences in an article.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Change in Data Sources Led to Lower Inflation Reading
item.description: A methodological change contributed to a better-than-expected inflation report, prompting questions from some economists.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.media:description: Changes to the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index are meaningful to the Federal Reserve, which targets the index for when making policy decisions.

item.title: Judge Quashes Justice Dept.’s Subpoenas of Fed, Crippling Its Pursuit of Trump’s Rivals
item.description: Judge James E. Boasberg derided the U.S. attorney’s office in Washington for pursuing a case against Jerome H. Powell that appeared to be motivated by President Trump’s desire for vengeance.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Glenn Thrush and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The government has offered no evidence whatsoever that Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, committed any crime other than displeasing the president,” Judge James E. Boasberg wrote.

item.title: Trump’s Move to Seize Oil Tankers Costs the U.S. Tens of Millions of Dollars
item.description: Although President Trump said seizing tankers would be a financial boon, the cost of maintaining just one aging ship has already reached $47 million.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:description: The oil tanker Skipper, seized by the United States, seen off the coast of Texas near Galveston in December.

item.title: Lewis E. Lehrman, Store Chain Heir Who Ran for N.Y. Governor, Dies at 87
item.description: After helping his family’s Rite-Aid drugstore empire flourish, he waged a surprisingly close but losing race as a Reagan Republican against Mario Cuomo in 1982.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: Gilder Lehrman Institute of American History
item.media:description: Mr. Lehrman campaigned on Broadway in Lower Manhattan in October 1982. On Election Day, he came close to winning, losing to Mr. Cuomo by 180,000 votes, or two percentage points.

item.title: How This Oil Supply Shock Compares With the Embargo of 1973
item.description: Governments have stockpiled oil, and cars are more efficient but the supply shock is global, and there’s no sense of when it’ll end.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The 1973 oil embargo led to panic buying, and long lines at gas stations. While that isn’t likely now in the United States, the price shock is likely to reverberate through the economy.

item.title: Pete Hegseth Says ‘the Sooner David Ellison’ Buys CNN, ‘the Better’
item.description: The remarks fueled concerns that CNN’s prospective new owner may shift its coverage in a Trump-friendly direction.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth’s remarks underscored concerns that David Ellison could seek to shift the network’s reporting in a more Trump-friendly direction.

item.title: The War in Iran Is Roiling the World, but Not the U.S. Stock Market
item.description: Investors say they are balancing the risks from oil disruptions against the many positives like strong corporate earnings and the likelihood that President Trump will end the war if it threatens markets.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Wall Street Bankers Offered Lucrative Access to Join the Pentagon
item.description: A presentation from a headhunting firm aimed to recruit Wall Street investors to the Pentagon by offering “unmatched access” to government officials and fund-raising opportunities among foreign sovereigns.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The Pentagon recruiting effort offers Wall Street investors the opportunity to manage “more capital than most investors deploy in their entire careers.”

item.title: U.S. Tech Giants Flocked to the Persian Gulf. Now They Are Targets.
item.description: Amazon, Google and others struck deals in the Persian Gulf to foot the bill for A.I. development. Iran has now threatened attacks against the companies’ infrastructure in the region.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Paul Mozur
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke rose after a reported Iranian drone strike on a fuel facility of Bahrain International Airport on Thursday. Iranian drones have also struck Amazon’s data center in Bahrain and two others in the U.A.E.

item.title: The War in Iran Is Driving Costs Up. Here Are Some Ways to Save.
item.description: The widening war in the Mideast is making life more expensive. Experts shared some simple cost-cutting tips.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: Gas prices have surged since the start of the war in Iran, with drivers in California paying the highest prices in the nation.

item.title: What to Do if You’re a Data Breach Victim (and You Probably Are)
item.description: Data incidents have become so common that you may be tempted to throw away notification letters. Don’t do that, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Consumer Prices Rose in January, Before Iran War Added Price Pressures
item.description: Economic growth at the end of 2025 was revised downward and consumer prices rose at the start of 2026.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.media:description: The latest price data offers a worrisome setup for inflation going forward.

item.title: Kevin Warsh’s Dilemma as Fed Chair Nominee Amid Iran War
item.description: President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve could faces a tough road to confirmation if he seeks quick interest rate cuts. The war in Iran doesn’t help.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Eli Tan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh, President Trump’s nominee for Fed chair, could inherit a central bank beset by challenges stemming from the war in Iran.

item.title: How Recovery Scams Retarget Victims of Fraud
item.description: Many cybercrime victims are retargeted by online scammers posing as lawyers or other professionals who promise they can recover stolen money.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nick Jonas requested a video call with a lawyer who said he could help his father, who had fallen victim to a scam. The lawyer was a fraud.

item.title: ‘A Lot of Life Years Lost’: How NAFTA Shortened American Life Spans
item.description: A study tracks how the North American Free Trade Agreement and trade competition with Mexico led to earlier deaths for American factory workers.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A steel mill in Braddock, Pa. Researchers found that the North American Free Trade Agreement sped up the decline of the U.S. manufacturing industry.

item.title: Honda Scraps Plans for E.V.s While Start-Ups Forge Ahead
item.description: The Japanese company’s retreat echoes moves by other traditional carmakers as the industry divides between electric vehicle haves and have-nots.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Honda once planned to retool its plant in Marysville, Ohio, to make electric vehicles. The company announced this week that it was scrapping plans for three battery-powered models.

item.title: How the Iran War Threatens the Global Food, Energy and Other Supplies
item.description: Beyond its effects on oil and gas, the unfolding war in the Middle East is roiling shipping and airfreight, threatening the availability of a vast range of goods.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The container terminal in Newark.

item.title: What to Know About the U.S. Lasers That Could Be Used to Counter Iranian Attacks
item.description: High-energy lasers can burn drones in the sky like a blowtorch. But they can be stymied by weather, and making them at scale will be a challenge.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.category: Laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation)
United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
item.media:description: A high-energy laser on display during a manufacturing conference in Detroit last year.

item.title: Surging Energy Costs Put German Industry ‘Really in Danger’
item.description: Four years after the Russian invasion of Ukraine sent energy prices soaring, the war in Iran is posing another challenge to efforts to revive European factories.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson and Andreas Meichsner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs

item.title: Oil Rises, Bringing Gains to 40% Since the Start of the War
item.description: After surging about 10 percent on Thursday, oil prices had little reaction to the decision by President Trump to waive sanctions on the sale of some Russian crude.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: War in Iran Has India Scrambling to Keep Stoves Lit for Cooking
item.description: India relies on huge quantities of cooking gas that is normally shipped from the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B. and Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Workers cooked food using firewood instead of gas outside a restaurant in Bengaluru, India, amid disruptions in the liquid petroleum gas supply.

item.title: With Disputed Legal Maneuver, Trump Tries to Set Policy Without Legislation
item.description: By suing Republican states and making sharp reversals in old cases, the Trump administration is using courts to fast-track major shifts in policy.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In some cases, the legal maneuver has helped the Trump administration lock in changes at the federal level that could persist for years after President Trump leaves office.

item.title: Trump Targets Forced Labor in Global Tariff Scheme
item.description: The Trump administration began a trade investigation Thursday into whether dozens of countries have policies to combat forced labor.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trucks leaving the Port of Los Angeles last May. The latest trade investigation is part of an effort by the Trump administration to resurrect a global system of tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court last month.

item.title: Banking Regulators Plan to Ease Some Capital Requirements
item.description: The Federal Reserve and other financial regulators will soon release proposals to loosen some planned rules on how much capital banks must hold in reserve to mitigate risks.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:description: Michelle W. Bowman, the Federal Reserve’s vice chair for supervision, last year.

item.title: The Jewelry Industry Is Taking a Wild Ride as Gold and Silver Prices Soar
item.description: Some jewelers are turning to wood, steel and even platinum. Others are leaning into the volatility with bigger and bolder designs.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Gomelsky

item.title: Noma Could Have Changed So Much More Than Food
item.description: The globally acclaimed restaurant had the power and influence to remake restaurant culture. Instead, it perpetuated the darkest parts of that world.
item.dc:creator: Tejal Rao
item.media:credit: Ditte Isager for The New York Times
item.media:description: Noma opened in 2003 in Copenhagen. Twenty years later, the restaurant announced that it would close to focus on other efforts, like the pop-up it started this week in Los Angeles,

item.title: Fallout From Iran War and Oil Shock Deliver Another Blow to World Economy
item.description: Countries already walloped by a breakdown of the international trading order, war in Ukraine and chaotic U.S. policymaking are facing potentially lasting economic damage.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo ships on Wednesday in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Senate Resoundingly Passes Housing Bill, but Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: The lopsided vote to approve the measure was a rare bit of election-year bipartisanship on a major affordability issue, but G.O.P. disputes and President Trump’s disinterest have left its fate uncertain.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Home construction in South Carolina earlier this year.

item.title: U.S. Mortgage Rates Rise, Undercutting Housing Affordability Push
item.description: Mortgage rates fell below the critical 6 percent threshold just a couple weeks ago. But they’re climbing again as new inflation concerns have roiled financial markets.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Home buyers have already been struggling with high prices, as well as rising insurance and property taxes.

item.title: War With Iran Puts Further Strain on America’s Pessimistic Farmers
item.description: Nearly 75 percent of farmers surveyed before the war said the crop sector was in a recession. They face bigger hurdles this planting season.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: Corn harvesting in Earlham, Iowa, last year. The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran is hurting U.S. farmers because the Middle East is crucial to worldwide fertilizer production.

item.title: Why Does Oil Matter So Much to the Global Economy?
item.description: The war in the Middle East has sent oil and gas prices soaring. Our energy reporter Rebecca F. Elliott breaks down how much the world continues to depend on reliable sources of oil.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Sutton Raphael, Joey Sendaydiego, James Surdam and Nikolay Nikolov

item.title: Iran War Causing Largest Ever Oil Disruption, I.E.A. Says
item.description: Conflict is forcing producers to slash production and close ports as Iran steps up attacks on energy infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: High prices at a gas station in Los Angeles on March 9, 2026, as costs have surged because of the conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Falls in January
item.description: The data showed imports dipped and exports rose in the month before the Supreme Court struck down most of the president’s tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A container terminal at the Port of Los Angeles.

item.title: Private Equity’s Private Credit Problem
item.description: Investors have worried about who would get hit if there’s a private credit bust. Many on Wall Street think the pain goes further than the lenders.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Many on Wall Street worry that lenders like Blue Owl Capital aren’t the only potential casualties of a private credit bust.

item.title: What to Know About Airport Security Lines During the Partial Government Shutdown
item.description: Images of seemingly endless waits at security checkpoints have spread online, but the reality of the partial government shutdown is less straightforward.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: The ongoing partial government shutdown could begin to have a bigger effect at Transportation Security Administration checkpoints after officers’ paychecks stop arriving on Friday.

item.title: Trump Administration Suggests Tariff Refunds May Take Significant Time
item.description: The government must update a federal court on Thursday about its timeline for returning roughly $166 billion in illegal duties.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: To refund any tariffs collected during the most punishing phase of President Trump’s trade war, the government said it would need more time.

item.title: Universal Will Give Movies Longer Exclusive Runs in Theaters
item.description: Theaters will get a minimum of five weekends of exclusive play and then, starting next year, seven. A pandemic-era policy was guaranteeing only three.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Coding After Coders: The End of Computer Programming as We Know It
item.description: In the era of A.I. agents, many Silicon Valley programmers are now barely programming. Instead, what they’re doing is deeply, deeply weird.
item.dc:creator: Clive Thompson
